text:
You made the decision. That already took courage.

Going back to school when you are older than most of your classmates is genuinely hard. You will notice the age gap. So will they. This discomfort fades faster than you expect.

Here is what actually matters: you are there because you chose to be, not because a parent or counselor steered you toward it. That changes everything about how you learn. You will ask questions others are afraid to ask. You will connect material to real experience. You will not waste time wondering why any of this is relevant, because you already know.

Some things will be harder. Technology moves fast and academic systems can feel foreign if you have been away from them. You may feel slow at first, or out of place in study groups. That feeling is information, not a verdict. Work through it.

Some things will be easier than you think. You know how to manage your time better than a twenty-year-old does. You understand that discomfort is temporary. You can tolerate ambiguity without panicking. These are significant advantages, and you probably undervalue them.

The hardest part is not the coursework. It is giving yourself permission to be a beginner again. Adults often protect themselves from looking inexperienced. School requires you to not know things and to say so out loud. Let that be fine.

You do not need to be the top student. You need to stay, keep showing up, and do the work. The credential matters, but so does the act of finishing what you started.

People will ask why you went back. The real answer is enough: you wanted something more, and you decided to go get it.

That is worth respecting. Including by you.
